title: Nitrogen Deficiency Crisis
description: Your tomato plant's lower leaves are turning yellow and growth has slowed. The plant is showing classic signs of nitrogen deficiency. Can you diagnose and fix the problem?
hints: Check the lower leaves - yellowing indicates nitrogen deficiency
Nitrogen is essential for leaf growth and green color
Optimal nitrogen range is 60-80%

educationalContent.explanation: Nitrogen is crucial for vegetative growth and chlorophyll production. Deficiency shows as yellowing of older leaves first, as the plant moves nitrogen to new growth.
educationalContent.solution: Apply nitrogen-rich fertilizer or compost to restore levels.

title: Overwatering Recovery
description: Your plant is wilting despite wet soil. The roots are struggling due to overwatering and lack of oxygen. Can you save the plant?
objectives: Increase container size to Medium or Large
hints: Too much water drowns roots and prevents oxygen uptake
Reduce watering and allow soil to dry slightly
Larger containers provide better drainage
Optimal water range is 50-75%

educationalContent.explanation: Overwatering is one of the most common mistakes. Roots need oxygen as well as water. Waterlogged soil prevents gas exchange and can lead to root rot.
educationalContent.solution: Allow soil to dry between waterings. Ensure good drainage with larger containers.

title: Acidic Soil Recovery
description: Your tomato plant shows yellowing leaves and stunted growth. Calcium and magnesium deficiency symptoms are visible. The soil pH is too low (acidic). Diagnose the problem and apply the correct treatment!
objectives: Identify that pH is too low (acidic)
hints: Yellowing leaves can indicate calcium/magnesium deficiency
Check the pH value - is it below 6.0?
Lime raises pH in acidic soil
Dolomite provides both calcium and magnesium

educationalContent.explanation: When soil pH is too low (acidic), calcium and magnesium become less available, causing deficiency symptoms. Tomatoes prefer pH 6.0-6.8.
educationalContent.solution: Apply lime (calcium carbonate) or dolomite (calcium + magnesium) to raise pH gradually.

title: Alkaline Soil & Nutrient Lockout
description: Your plant shows iron deficiency (yellowing between leaf veins) despite adequate nutrients. High pH is causing nutrient lockout. Diagnose and fix the problem!
objectives: Identify that pH is too high (alkaline)
Apply iron chelate to treat deficiency
hints: Yellowing between veins indicates iron deficiency
High pH (alkaline) locks out iron and micronutrients
Sulfur lowers pH in alkaline soil
Iron chelate treats symptoms while pH adjusts

educationalContent.explanation: At high pH (above 7.0), iron, manganese, and phosphorus become unavailable even if present in soil. This is called nutrient lockout.
educationalContent.solution: Apply elemental sulfur to lower pH. Use iron chelate for immediate symptom relief.

title: Small Space Optimization
description: You're growing tomatoes on a small balcony with limited space. Your plant is in a small pot and struggling. Can you optimize the conditions despite space constraints?
objectives: Optimize all nutrient levels for small container
Bring health above 75% despite small pot
hints: Small containers dry out faster and have limited nutrients
Fertilize more frequently in small pots
Focus on optimizing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium

educationalContent.explanation: Small containers limit root growth and nutrient reserves. They require more frequent watering and fertilization. However, they're perfect for balconies and small spaces.
educationalContent.solution: Use high-quality potting mix, fertilize regularly, and monitor moisture closely.

title: Balanced Nutrition Challenge
description: Your plant is entering the flowering stage and needs balanced NPK nutrition. Too much nitrogen will promote leaves over flowers. Can you achieve the perfect balance?
objectives: Reduce nitrogen to 60-70% (avoid excess vegetative growth)
hints: During flowering, reduce N and increase P and K
Phosphorus promotes root and flower development
Potassium improves fruit quality and disease resistance

educationalContent.explanation: Plant nutrient needs change through growth stages. Flowering and fruiting plants need less nitrogen (which promotes leaves) and more phosphorus and potassium for flowers and fruits.
educationalContent.solution: Use bloom fertilizers (lower N, higher P-K ratio) during flowering stage.
